    API->>Inv: confirm hold → COMMIT
    API->>K: booking.confirmed
    API-->>G: 201 confirmed
body.caption: Happy-path sequence from hold through payment to confirmed booking.
Booking saga with compensation

body.title: In practice
body.text: Expedia and Booking.com pipelines mirror this: Elasticsearch for discovery, PostgreSQL for reservations, Stripe/Adyen for payments, Temporal or custom sagas for multi-step checkout.

voting-requirements.title: Integrity and Trust
voting-requirements.summary: Voting systems must prevent duplicate ballots, resist tampering, and survive traffic spikes on election night.
keyTakeaways: One voter → one ballot: unique constraint on (election_id, voter_id).
Ballots should be append-only; tallies derived, not edited in place.
Secret ballot vs verifiability is a product/legal trade-off.
Rate limiting and bot detection at the edge.
Audit logs: who cast when (metadata), not necessarily how they voted.
Read scaling for results pages; write spike at poll close.

body.text: Unlike likes on a post, votes are legally and socially sensitive. The architecture must make certain attacks impossible (double vote) and others detectable (ballot box stuffing at scale).

body.title: Threat model first
body.text: Define adversaries: duplicate voter, admin tampering, DDoS, network partition during close. Each drives different controls.

body.title: In practice
body.text: Estonia's i-Voting uses cryptographic protocols; US precinct systems often air-gap tallies. Product web polls (Twitter, Slack) use idempotent voter keys and Redis rate limits — weaker but appropriate for low stakes.

voting-architecture.title: Ballots, Tallies, and Audits
voting-architecture.summary: Append ballots to an immutable log; aggregate counts in stream processors or materialized views.
keyTakeaways: Ballot ingest API validates voter eligibility once, writes immutable record.
Hash chain or signed events support third-party audit (high-stakes systems).
Tally service aggregates by choice_id; real-time preview uses stream processor.
Results page reads materialized count — never COUNT(*) on raw ballots live.
Idempotency-Key per voter session prevents double submit on retry.
Geo-distributed read replicas for results; single-region write primary for consistency.

body.source:
flowchart LR Voter[Voter] API["Ballot API
idempotent"] Log[("Ballot Log
append-only")] Agg["Aggregator
stream"] Results[("Results
cached")] Voter -->|POST ballot| API API -->|INSERT| Log API -->|ballot.cast| Agg Agg -->|INCR| Results Voter -.->|GET /results| Results
body.caption: Ballot API, append-only log, stream aggregator, and cached results.

body.source:
sequenceDiagram
    participant V as Voter
    participant API as Ballot API
    participant DB as Ballot Log
    participant Agg as Aggregator
    participant C as Results Cache
    V->>API: POST /ballots + Idempotency-Key
    API->>DB: INSERT ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING
    DB-->>API: ballotId
    API->>Agg: ballot.cast event
    API-->>V: 201 accepted
    Note over Agg,C: async tally
    Agg->>C: INCR choice_count
    V->>C: GET /results
    C-->>V: live totals
body.caption: Idempotent vote submission and async tally update to results cache.
Idempotent ballot submission

body.title: Slack at scale
body.text: Workspace polls are low-stakes but still use one-vote-per-user keys stored in OLTP with unique indexes — the same pattern at national scale with harder identity proofing.
Why these technologies?

body.title: Why PostgreSQL (append-only ballot log)?
body.text: UNIQUE (election_id, voter_id) enforces one vote per person at the database layer. Append-only inserts preserve audit trail; tallies are derived, never UPDATE-in-place on ballot rows.

body.title: Why Idempotency-Key header?
body.text: Network retries must not create duplicate ballots — same key returns the same result. Simpler than distributed locks for voter sessions.

body.title: Why Kafka / stream processor?
body.text: Aggregates millions of ballots into live totals without scanning the full table on every results page refresh. Handles write spikes when polls close.

body.title: Why Redis / materialized results cache?
body.text: Results pages are read-heavy during election night — precomputed counts serve sub-ms reads. Eventual consistency of +1–2 seconds is usually acceptable for display.

body.title: Why API gateway + rate limiting (Cloudflare / WAF)?
body.text: Bot protection and per-IP throttling at the edge before ballots hit your origin. Critical when viral links drive traffic spikes.

body.title: Why Not updating vote counts in the ballot row?
body.text: In-place UPDATE loses auditability and races under concurrency. Event sourcing pattern: insert ballot, derive count.

multiplayer-requirements.title: Latency and Fairness
multiplayer-requirements.summary: FPS and battle royale players feel 50 ms; turn-based tolerates seconds. Pick transport and authority model accordingly.
keyTakeaways: Sub-100 ms RTT often requires regional servers and UDP-based protocols.
Authoritative server prevents most cheating; client prediction hides latency.
Tick rate (20–128 Hz) trades CPU for smoothness.
Matchmaking groups similar skill and low ping — data problem + queue system.
Disconnect/reconnect must restore state from server snapshot.
Anti-cheat inspects impossible state transitions server-side.

body.text: Games are distributed systems with humans in the loop. Fairness means everyone sees a consistent world; responsiveness means hiding network delay with prediction and reconciliation.

body.title: In practice
body.text: Riot Games deploys regional shards (NA, EUW) with dedicated UDP servers. Among Us uses simpler authoritative state with lower tick demands. Protocol choice follows latency budget.

multiplayer-architecture.title: Game Servers and State Sync
multiplayer-architecture.summary: Dedicated simulation loop owns truth; clients send inputs, receive state deltas.
keyTakeaways: Lobby service assigns players to a game server instance.
Server sim loop: inputs in → physics/rules → state snapshot out.
Delta compression sends only changed entities to clients.
Redis or in-memory store for session; periodic checkpoint to DB for stats.
Dedicated servers vs P2P: commercial titles choose servers for anti-cheat.
Agones on Kubernetes scales game server pods per match.

body.source:
flowchart TB PA["Player A
UDP"] PB["Player B
UDP"] MM[Matchmaker] GS["Game Server
60 Hz tick"] K8s["Agones / K8s"] Tel[[Kafka telemetry]] PA -->|enqueue| MM PB -->|enqueue| MM MM -->|allocate pod| K8s K8s --> GS PA <-->|UDP input/state| GS PB <-->|UDP input/state| GS GS -.-> Tel
body.caption: Matchmaker, Agones/K8s game pods, and UDP state sync.

body.source:
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Player
    participant MM as Matchmaker
    participant K8s as Agones/K8s
    participant GS as Game Server
    P->>MM: enqueue(ticket)
    Note over MM: skill + region bucket
    MM->>K8s: allocate pod

body.source:
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Player A
    participant S as Game Server
    participant B as Player B
    A->>S: INPUT tick=42 (UDP)
    B->>S: INPUT tick=42 (UDP)

body.caption: Clients send inputs; server simulates and broadcasts state deltas.
Authoritative tick with input buffer

body.title: Fortnite at scale
body.text: Epic runs regional game server fleets; matchmaking minimizes RTT. Client sends inputs; server corrects position if client prediction diverges.
Why these technologies?

body.title: Why UDP (game traffic)?
body.text: TCP head-of-line blocking adds latency unsuitable for 60 Hz shooters. UDP drops packets — game state corrects on next tick. TCP/HTTP fine for lobby and matchmaking.

body.title: Why Authoritative game server?
body.text: Server simulates truth; clients predict locally then reconcile. Prevents speed hacks and wall hacks — P2P cannot enforce fairness in competitive games.

body.title: Why Matchmaker service?
body.text: Groups players by skill (MMR) and region (ping). Separate from game simulation — queue logic changes without redeploying game binaries.

body.title: Why Agones / Kubernetes game pods?
body.text: Each match is an isolated process with dedicated CPU — natural scale unit. Spin up pod per match, tear down after. StatefulSet assigns stable UDP ports.

body.title: Why In-memory state (not Postgres during match)?
body.text: Sub-16 ms tick loops cannot round-trip to disk. Checkpoint stats to DB only after match ends. Redis optional for cross-service lobby state.

body.title: Why Kafka (telemetry)?
body.text: Fire-and-forget analytics: kills, crashes, latency histograms. Never blocks the game tick loop.

body.title: Why Not a single global game database?
body.text: Game state is ephemeral and regional — sharding by match ID, not user ID.

multiplayer-scaling.title: Sharding and Matchmaking
multiplayer-scaling.summary: Scale horizontally by adding game server processes; matchmaking is the control plane.
keyTakeaways: Each match is an isolated process — natural parallelism boundary.
Matchmaking queues partition by mode/region/rank.
Backpressure when server pool full: queue wait UX vs degraded bots.
Cross-region play only when product accepts high ping.
Telemetry pipeline (Kafka) feeds ranking and crash analytics.
Stateful sets or Agones allocate UDP ports per pod.

body.title: In practice
body.text: Call of Duty warzone spins thousands of single-match processes across cloud VMs. Control plane tracks capacity; data plane is ephemeral per round.

body.caption: Regional matchmaking queue

puzzle-turn-model.title: Turns, Timers, and Async Play
puzzle-turn-model.summary: Wordle, chess, and daily puzzles mix turn order, deadlines, and optional async moves.
keyTakeaways: Turn state machine: WAITING → ACTIVE → COMPLETED per player.
Server validates moves against rules before applying to board.
Timers use scheduled jobs (SQS delay, Redis TTL) to forfeit on timeout.
Async mode: push notification when opponent moves.
Daily puzzles: same seed globally; validate once per user per day.
Smaller payloads than FPS — HTTPS and WebSocket both fine.

body.text: Puzzle games stress correctness over microseconds. A illegal word guess must be rejected; a daily puzzle must not accept two submissions from the same account.

body.title: Wordle at scale
body.text: One puzzle ID per calendar day, stored as immutable answer hash. Client submits guess; server returns tile feedback without leaking future answers.

body.caption: Daily puzzle idempotency

puzzle-conflicts.title: Merging Moves and Leaderboards
puzzle-conflicts.summary: Concurrent edits to shared boards need versioning; leaderboards need approximate top-K at scale.
keyTakeaways: Board state version increments on each valid move; stale versions rejected.
Shared puzzles (collaborative crosswords) may use CRDT grids for cells.
Leaderboards: Redis sorted sets for real-time; batch reconcile for seasons.
Anti-cheat: server-side dictionary and move legality only.
Spectator mode reads immutable move log — same pattern as event sourcing.
Tie-breakers defined upfront (time, fewer hints).

body.source:
flowchart LR Player[Player] API["Game API
validate"] Log[("Move Log")] Board[("Board DB
versioned")] Redis[("Redis ZSET
leaderboard")] Timer[Scheduler] Player -->|POST /move| API API -->|append| Log API -->|UPDATE version| Board API -.->|ZADD| Redis Timer -.->|forfeit| API
body.caption: Move log, versioned board, and Redis leaderboard aggregation.

body.source:
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Player
    participant API as Game API
    participant B as Board DB
    participant L as Move Log
    participant R as Redis
    P->>API: POST /move v=7
    API->>B: UPDATE … WHERE version=7
    B-->>API: 1 row (ok)
    API->>L: append move event
    API->>R: ZADD score
    API-->>P: 200 new board
body.caption: Validated move with optimistic locking, event log, and score update.
Optimistic board version check

body.title: In practice
body.text: Chess.com stores PGN move logs; lichess uses similar event-sourced game records. NYT Games leaderboard uses precomputed daily ranks to avoid scanning all players at read time.
Why these technologies?

body.title: Why PostgreSQL (board state)?
body.text: Turn-based games tolerate 50–200 ms latency — ACID updates with version column prevent stale moves. Simpler than CRDTs for single-writer-per-turn chess.

body.title: Why Append-only move log?
body.text: Replay games, spectator mode, and dispute resolution. Event sourcing lite — board is a projection of moves.

body.title: Why Redis sorted sets (leaderboard)?
body.text: O(log N) rank updates and top-K queries at millions of players. Recomputing ranks in SQL nightly is too slow for live daily puzzles.

body.title: Why Scheduler (SQS delay / Redis TTL / cron)?
body.text: Forfeit players who exceed turn timeout without polling every second in application code.

body.title: Why HTTPS / WebSocket (not UDP)?
body.text: Puzzle games are not latency-competitive — reliable delivery matters more. JSON payloads are tiny.

body.title: Why Push notifications (APNs/FCM)?
body.text: Async turn games notify opponent it's their move — optional but standard for retention.

multistep-patterns.title: Sagas and Orchestration
multistep-patterns.summary: Long-running flows span services. Sagas coordinate forward steps and compensating rollbacks.
keyTakeaways: Choreography: each service emits events; others react (loose coupling).
Orchestration: central workflow engine calls services in order (clear visibility).
Each step idempotent; correlation ID ties the saga together.
Compensating transactions undo prior steps (cancel shipment, refund hold).
Timeouts trigger automatic compensation or human escalation.
Shopify checkout and Uber trip lifecycle are canonical sagas.

body.text: A multistep checkout is not one database transaction — payment, inventory, shipping, and email span systems. Sagas make partial failure explicit instead of hoping for the best.

body.caption: Saga step with compensation

body.source:
flowchart LR Client[Client] Orch["Orchestrator
Temporal"] Inv[Inventory] Pay[Payments] Ship[Shipping] Client --> Orch Orch -->|reserve| Inv Orch -->|capture| Pay Orch -->|create| Ship Pay -.->|FAILED| Orch Orch -.->|compensate release| Inv
body.caption: Orchestrator coordinates inventory, payments, and shipping services.

body.source:
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant O as Orchestrator
    participant I as Inventory
    participant P as Payments
    participant S as Shipping
    C->>O: start checkout
    O->>I: reserve stock
    I-->>O: holdId
    O->>P: capture payment
    P-->>O: chargeId
    O->>S: create shipment
    S-->>O: trackingId
    O-->>C: order complete
body.caption: Forward saga steps from cart to shipment with durable orchestration.

body.source:
sequenceDiagram
    participant O as Orchestrator
    participant I as Inventory
    participant P as Payments
    O->>I: reserve stock
    I-->>O: holdId
    O->>P: capture payment
    P-->>O: FAILED
    O->>I: release hold (compensate)
    I-->>O: stock restored
body.caption: Payment failure triggers compensating release of inventory hold.

body.title: In practice
body.text: Temporal, AWS Step Functions, and Cadence power durable workflows at Uber, Netflix, and Stripe. Kafka-only choreography works until you need a human-readable workflow graph.
Why these technologies?

body.title: Why Temporal / Step Functions / Cadence?
body.text: Durable workflow state survives worker crashes. Timers (wait 7 days for KYC) without holding threads. Replay history for debugging failed checkouts.

body.title: Why Saga pattern (not 2PC)?
body.text: Payment, inventory, and shipping are separate services — XA transactions across Stripe and your DB don't exist. Compensating actions (release hold) are explicit.

body.title: Why Kafka (choreography option)?
body.text: Services react to events without a central orchestrator — looser coupling, harder to visualize. Many teams start with orchestration, add events for notifications.

body.title: Why Per-service PostgreSQL?
body.text: Each bounded context owns its data — inventory service DB, payment service ledger. Shared monolith DB creates deployment coupling.

body.title: Why Idempotent activity implementations?
body.text: Retries are guaranteed — charge API must accept same idempotency key, ship API must tolerate duplicate create with same correlation ID.

body.title: Why Dead-letter queue?
body.text: Poison messages (bad payload) isolate without blocking the whole saga — ops can inspect and replay.

multistep-durability.title: Durable Execution and Retries
multistep-durability.summary: Workflow state must survive process crashes. Durable timers and replayable history are the answer.
keyTakeaways: Workflow engine persists event history before calling external APIs.
Activities retry with exponential backoff; non-retryable errors branch to compensate.
Exactly-once illusion via idempotent activity implementations.
Human tasks (KYC review) park workflow for days without holding threads.
Visibility APIs show ops which step failed and with what payload.
Version workflows carefully — running instances use old definition.

body.title: Uber at scale
body.text: A trip saga spans matching, driver en route, payment capture, and receipt email. Temporal replays from history if the worker crashes mid-step.

body.caption: Durable workflow stub

body.title: Outbox + inbox
body.text: Combine sagas with transactional outbox so 'charge card' and 'mark order paid' never diverge.

multistep-examples.title: Real-World Workflow Stacks
multistep-examples.summary: Compare how production companies implement the same pattern with different engines.
keyTakeaways: E-commerce checkout: cart → hold inventory → charge → fulfill → notify.
Onboarding KYC: upload ID → vendor verify → manual review → activate.
Loan origination: apply → credit pull → underwrite → fund → servicer handoff.
Each step is a service boundary; workflow engine is the spine.
Observability: trace ID per saga instance across all HTTP and queue hops.
Dead-letter queues capture poison steps without losing the saga context.

body.title: In practice
body.text: Stripe Checkout is a hosted multistep UI over PaymentIntents sagas. Airbnb host onboarding chains identity, payout, and listing steps with manual gates. Banks use Camunda or custom mainframes with the same state-machine thinking.

collaboration-presence.title: Presence and Permissions
collaboration-presence.summary: See who is online, what they are editing, and whether they may change it — without leaking document content.
keyTakeaways: Presence heartbeats in Redis with TTL; disconnect clears avatar.
Document ACLs: owner, editor, commenter, viewer enforced on every op.
Cursors and selections are ephemeral broadcast, not durable history.
Share links map tokens to roles with expiration.
Audit log records permission changes, not every keystroke.
Google Docs 'anonymous animals' are presence UX on top of WebSocket fan-out.

body.text: Collaboration tools combine real-time transport with authorization. A user without edit permission must not apply operations even if they bypass the UI.

body.title: In practice
body.text: Figma stores file permissions in Postgres; presence in Redis. Notion blocks embed share settings per page tree. All validate ops server-side against ACL snapshot.

body.caption: Presence heartbeat

collaboration-sync.title: OT, CRDTs, and Conflict-Free Editing
collaboration-sync.summary: Concurrent edits merge without a single global lock. OT needs a server; CRDTs can peer-sync.
keyTakeaways: Operational Transformation: server transforms concurrent ops against each other.
CRDTs: mathematically merge without central coordinator (Yjs, Automerge).
Choose OT for rich text with complex intent; CRDTs for JSON/tree structures.
Periodic snapshots bound replay time on reconnect.
Comment threads attach to stable anchors (block IDs), not byte offsets.
Offline edit queues flush on reconnect with merge.

collaboration-architecture.title: Storage and Real-Time Transport
collaboration-architecture.summary: Split blob storage, metadata DB, collab server, and CDN for assets.
keyTakeaways: Object storage (S3) for images/fonts; Postgres for doc metadata and ACL.
Collab server cluster sticky by document ID for OT ordering.
WebSocket gateway scales with connection count; Redis pub/sub bridges regions.
Export/render jobs async — separate from live editing path.
Version history: snapshot + op log compaction weekly.
Same architecture pattern as Canva, Google Docs, Notion — different CRDT/OT choices.

body.title: In practice
body.text: Google Docs historically used centralized OT servers at scale. Notion shards documents across cells; Miro uses regional collab routers for EU data residency.
Why these technologies?

body.title: Why WebSocket collab server?
body.text: Sub-100 ms operation broadcast for cursors and edits. HTTP polling would feel laggy and waste bandwidth.

body.title: Why OT or CRDT library (Yjs, Automerge)?
body.text: Merge concurrent edits without locking the whole document. OT needs server ordering; CRDTs can peer-sync offline then merge.

body.title: Why PostgreSQL (metadata & ACL)?
body.text: Document titles, share permissions, folder hierarchy — relational model fits. Not for multi-MB canvas blobs.

body.title: Why S3 / GCS (assets)?
body.text: Images, fonts, video layers are large immutable files — cheap object storage with CDN in front for export/download.

body.title: Why Redis (presence & pub/sub)?
body.text: Who is online, cursor color, typing — ephemeral with TTL. Pub/sub bridges collab server instances in multi-region deploys.

body.title: Why Sticky routing by document ID?
body.text: OT requires consistent operation ordering per document — route all ops for doc X to the same server or use CRDT to avoid stickiness.

body.title: Why Not storing every keystroke in Postgres forever?
body.text: Op log compacted to periodic snapshots — unbounded insert rate would saturate OLTP.

forms-state-machine.title: Steps, Drafts, and Validation
forms-state-machine.summary: Wizards span sessions and devices. Persist partial progress; validate per step and on submit.
keyTakeaways: Explicit step graph: some steps skippable based on prior answers.
Draft record keyed by user + form version; autosave debounced.
Server-side validation authoritative — client hints are UX only.
Schema versioning: in-flight drafts pinned to form version they started.
Resume token in email link maps to draft without exposing PII in URL.
Final submit is idempotent — duplicate clicks do not create two applications.

body.text: Mortgage and insurance forms take days. Users expect to pause on mobile and finish on desktop. That requires durable draft storage, not session cookies.

body.caption: Multistep draft autosave

body.title: Stripe at scale
body.text: Connect onboarding is a hosted multistep form with saved progress — merchants resume KYC without re-entering verified fields.

forms-architecture.title: Backend for Long-Running Applications
forms-architecture.summary: API design for partial saves, file uploads, and async verification steps.
keyTakeaways: POST /drafts upserts JSON blob; PATCH /steps/:id validates slice only.
Large uploads direct-to-S3 with signed URLs; draft stores pointer.
Async steps (credit check) transition draft to PENDING_VERIFICATION.
Webhook or poll updates UI when vendor returns.
PII encrypted at rest; field-level encryption for SSN/tax IDs.
Analytics on funnel drop-off per step drives product iteration.

body.title: In practice
body.text: TurboTax and government visa portals use identical patterns: session-less drafts in DB, step validators as pure functions, async steps wired to mainframe or vendor APIs.

body.caption: Step validation handler

body.text: Why these technologies?

body.title: Why PostgreSQL (draft storage)?
body.text: Multi-day forms need durable JSON blobs keyed by user + form version — survives browser close and device switch. Session cookies expire; DB drafts don't.

body.title: Why S3 pre-signed URLs?
body.text: Large documents (PDFs, scans) upload directly to object storage — API servers don't proxy gigabyte files. Draft row stores S3 key pointer only.

body.title: Why Step-scoped validation API?
body.text: Validate only fields for current step — faster UX and clearer errors than validating entire 50-field form on every keystroke.

body.title: Why Async verification vendor + webhook?
body.text: Credit checks and identity verification take seconds to days — cannot block HTTP request. Webhook transitions draft from PENDING to APPROVED.

body.title: Why Idempotent final submit?
body.text: Double-click submit must not create two loan applications — same pattern as Stripe idempotency keys.

body.title: Why Field-level encryption?
body.text: SSN and tax IDs encrypted at rest in Postgres — defense in depth beyond disk encryption.

body.title: Why Not a workflow engine for simple 3-step forms?
body.text: Temporal is overkill for a checkout wizard — Postgres draft + state machine in application code suffices until human review gates appear.
